content: read reviews/103.md. if the summary section of the checklist review indicates the review passed, do nothing. if it failed, make relevant updates that are needed to deepmind/103.lean accordingly. if in doubt, reference CHECKLIST.md, which was the source document used when a previous instance of claude produced reviews/103.md.

    27→

    29→  - `IsUnitSeparated` (line 30–33)
    30→  - `finiteDiameter` (line 37–39)
    31→  - `IsMinimalDiameter` (line 43–47)
    32→  - `AreCongruent` (line 53–57)
    33→  - `h` (line 63–66)
    34→- [x] Definitions involving nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`: `finiteDiameter` (uses `sSup`) and `h` (uses `Set.ncard`).
    35→- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
    36→- [x] Types/Props use `UpperCamelCase`: `IsUnitSeparated`, `IsMinimalDiameter`, `AreCongruent`.
    37→- [x] Functions returning values use `lowerCamelCase`: `finiteDiameter`, `h`.
    38→- [ ] N/A — No `local notation` used (not needed for this problem).
    39→- [ ] N/A — No `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks on definitions (optional but could be considered for verifying, e.g., that `AreCongruent` is reflexive).
    40→
    41→## 6. Theorem Statements
    42→
    43→- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
    44→- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_103` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
    45→- [x] Name follows Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions.
    46→- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 52]`.
    47→- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`, lines 73–80).
    48→- [x] Docstring references the source problem ("Erdős Problem #103") and includes additional context about what is known.
    49→
    50→### `answer()` usage
    51→
    52→The problem asks "Is it true that h(n) → ∞?" — this is a **yes/no question** (Case 1).
    53→
    54→- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P`.
    55→- [x] RHS correctly formalizes h(n) → ∞: `∀ M : ℕ, ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N → h n ≥ M`.
    56→- [x] `answer(True)` would mean "yes, h(n) → ∞" and `answer(False)` would mean "no" — matches the question as asked.
    57→- [x] Quantifiers are **after** `answer(sorry)`, not as theorem parameters.
    58→
    59→## 7. Category Attribute
    60→
    61→- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute: `category research open`.
    62→- [x] Value is valid (`research open`).
    63→- [x] `research open` theorem contains `sorry` in its proof body.
    64→
    65→## 8. AMS Attribute
    66→
    67→- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 52`.
    68→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed.
    69→- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single number).
    70→- [x] No duplicates.
    71→- [x] `52` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Convex and discrete geometry).
    72→
    73→## 9. Proof Bodies
    74→
    75→- [x] Uses `by` / `sorry` (multi-line form: `by\n  sorry` — acceptable per checklist).
    76→- [x] No `sorry` in any `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declaration.
    77→- [x] No informal proof sketches for this open problem.
    78→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    79→
    80→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
    81→
    82→- N/A — No variants present. The file contains a single main theorem.
    83→
    84→## 11. Code Quality
    85→
    86→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    87→- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable: `≥`, `ℝ`, `ℕ`, `∀`, `∃`, `↔`, `≃ᵢ`, `∈`, `∧`, `≠`.
    88→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    89→- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
    90→- [x] Lines are reasonably short (longest line is ~82 characters, well under 100).
    91→- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.
    92→
    93→## 12. Build Verification
    94→
    95→- [ ] Not verified — requires running `lake build` locally.
    96→
    97→---
    98→
    99→## Mathematical Faithfulness Notes
   100→
   101→The formalization is mathematically sound:
   102→
   103→- **h(n) → ∞** is correctly encoded as `∀ M, ∃ N, ∀ n ≥ N, h n ≥ M` (standard ε-δ style divergence to infinity for ℕ-valued sequences).
   104→- **AreCongruent** uses `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) ≃ᵢ EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`, which includes all isometries (rotations, reflections, translations), matching the standard notion of congruence.
   105→- **h** counts congruence classes via `Set.ncard` on the quotient-like construction `{C | ∃ A ∈ minimizers, C = minimizers ∩ {B | AreCongruent A B}}`. This is well-defined since `AreCongruent` is an equivalence relation (reflexive via identity, symmetric via inverse, transitive via composition of isometries).
   106→- **IsMinimalDiameter** correctly requires both unit separation and diameter-minimality among all same-cardinality unit-separated sets.
   107→
   108→## Verdict
   109→
   110→**PASS** — All applicable checklist items are satisfied. The file is clean, well-structured, and mathematically faithful to the original problem.
   111→

file.content:
# Review: deepmind/103.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line (`import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`), immediately after the copyright block, separated by one blank line.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 103`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `[erdosproblems.com/103](https://www.erdosproblems.com/103)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (defines h(n) and asks whether h(n) → ∞).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos103 ... end Erdos103`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos103`).
- [x] No `open` / `open scoped` statements are present (none needed; no external Mathlib namespaces are opened).

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] All helper definitions have LaTeX docstrings (`/-- ... -/`):
  - `IsUnitSeparated` (line 30–33)
  - `finiteDiameter` (line 37–39)
  - `IsMinimalDiameter` (line 43–47)
  - `AreCongruent` (line 53–57)
  - `h` (line 63–66)
- [x] Definitions involving nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`: `finiteDiameter` (uses `sSup`) and `h` (uses `Set.ncard`).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] Types/Props use `UpperCamelCase`: `IsUnitSeparated`, `IsMinimalDiameter`, `AreCongruent`.
- [x] Functions returning values use `lowerCamelCase`: `finiteDiameter`, `h`.
- [ ] N/A — No `local notation` used (not needed for this problem).
- [ ] N/A — No `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks on definitions (optional but could be considered for verifying, e.g., that `AreCongruent` is reflexive).

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_103` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
- [x] Name follows Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` conventions.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 52]`.
- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`, lines 73–80).
- [x] Docstring references the source problem ("Erdős Problem #103") and includes additional context about what is known.

### `answer()` usage

The problem asks "Is it true that h(n) → ∞?" — this is a **yes/no question** (Case 1).

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P`.
- [x] RHS correctly formalizes h(n) → ∞: `∀ M : ℕ, ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N → h n ≥ M`.
- [x] `answer(True)` would mean "yes, h(n) → ∞" and `answer(False)` would mean "no" — matches the question as asked.
- [x] Quantifiers are **after** `answer(sorry)`, not as theorem parameters.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute: `category research open`.
- [x] Value is valid (`research open`).
- [x] `research open` theorem contains `sorry` in its proof body.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 52`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single number).
- [x] No duplicates.
- [x] `52` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Convex and discrete geometry).

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses `by` / `sorry` (multi-line form: `by\n  sorry` — acceptable per checklist).
- [x] No `sorry` in any `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declaration.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for this open problem.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A — No variants present. The file contains a single main theorem.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable: `≥`, `ℝ`, `ℕ`, `∀`, `∃`, `↔`, `≃ᵢ`, `∈`, `∧`, `≠`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (longest line is ~82 characters, well under 100).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] Not verified — requires running `lake build` locally.

---

## Mathematical Faithfulness Notes

The formalization is mathematically sound:

- **h(n) → ∞** is correctly encoded as `∀ M, ∃ N, ∀ n ≥ N, h n ≥ M` (standard ε-δ style divergence to infinity for ℕ-valued sequences).
- **AreCongruent** uses `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) ≃ᵢ EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`, which includes all isometries (rotations, reflections, translations), matching the standard notion of congruence.
- **h** counts congruence classes via `Set.ncard` on the quotient-like construction `{C | ∃ A ∈ minimizers, C = minimizers ∩ {B | AreCongruent A B}}`. This is well-defined since `AreCongruent` is an equivalence relation (reflexive via identity, symmetric via inverse, transitive via composition of isometries).
- **IsMinimalDiameter** correctly requires both unit separation and diameter-minimality among all same-cardinality unit-separated sets.

## Verdict

**PASS** — All applicable checklist items are satisfied. The file is clean, well-structured, and mathematically faithful to the original problem.

content.text: The review passed with a **PASS** verdict. No changes needed.
